examples.explanation: Step 1: Start from vertex 0. DFS: 0->1 (depth 1), 1->2 (depth 2), 2->3 (depth 3), 3->0 (depth 4) - found cycle! Cycle length = depth(3) + depth(0) + 1 = 3 + 0 + 1 = 4. But 3 is already visited from 0? Wait, need to check: when at 3 (depth 3), neighbor 0 is visited but not parent (parent is 2), so cycle length = depth[3] + depth[0] + 1 = 3 + 0 + 1 = 4. Update min_cycle = 4. Continue DFS from 0: also path 0->3 (depth 1), 3->1 (depth 2), 1->2 (depth 3), 2->3 (depth 4) but 3 visited (parent 1) so cycle length = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7. Not shorter. Step 2: Start from vertex 1. DFS: 1->0 (depth 1), 0->3 (depth 2), 3->2 (depth 3), 2->1 (depth 4) - found cycle! 1 is visited (parent 0), so cycle length = depth[2] + depth[1] + 1 = 3 + 0 + 1 = 4. Also path 1->3 (depth 1), 3->0 (depth 2), 0->1 (depth 3) - cycle length = 2 + 0 + 1 = 3. Update min_cycle = 3. Step 3: Start from vertices 2 and 3 will find same cycles. Final min_cycle = 3.

Step 1: Start from vertex 0. DFS: 0->1 (depth 1), 1->2 (depth 2). No cycles found. Step 2: Start from vertex 1. DFS: 1->0 (depth 1), 1->2 (depth 1). No cycles found. Step 3: Start from vertex 2. DFS: 2->1 (depth 1), 1->0 (depth 2). No cycles found. All DFS complete, no cycles detected. Return -1.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS
approaches.summary: For each vertex, perform DFS to find cycles starting from it, tracking visited vertices and parent nodes to avoid revisiting edges. This approach is straightforward but inefficient for large graphs due to repeated traversals.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force DFS
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of finding the shortest cycle length in an undirected graph using DFS from each vertex.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution to find the shortest cycle in an undirected graph using a brute force DFS approach. The intuition is that any cycle must start and end at some vertex, so by performing DFS from each vertex as a potential starting point, we can detect cycles and track their lengths. For each vertex v, we perform DFS to find cycles that include v, keeping track of visited vertices and parent nodes to avoid revisiting edges. We maintain a global minimum cycle length that gets updated whenever a cycle is found. The algorithm works by exploring all possible cycles in the graph, but it's inefficient because it repeats work - the same cycle might be discovered multiple times from different starting vertices. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize min_cycle = INF. 2. For each vertex v in graph: a. Perform DFS from v to find cycles containing v. b. Track visited vertices and parent to avoid going back along the same edge. c. When we encounter an already visited vertex that's not the parent, we've found a cycle. d. Calculate cycle length as current depth + depth of visited vertex. e. Update min_cycle if this cycle is shorter. 3. Return min_cycle or -1 if no cycle exists. Time complexity is O(V * (V + E)) because we perform DFS from each vertex, and each DFS takes O(V + E) time. Space complexity is O(V) for visited array and recursion stack. This connects to L1 by using the DFS cycle detection as a subroutine for each vertex. Common mistakes include: forgetting to mark vertices as unvisited after DFS from each starting vertex, incorrectly calculating cycle length, and not handling the case where there's no cycle. The real code pattern involves nested loops with DFS recursion.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start from vertex 0. DFS: 0->1 (depth 1), 1->2 (depth 2), 2->3 (depth 3), 3->0 (depth 4) - found cycle! Cycle length = depth(3) + depth(0) + 1 = 3 + 0 + 1 = 4. But 3 is already visited from 0? Wait, need to check: when at 3 (depth 3), neighbor 0 is visited but not parent (parent is 2), so cycle length = depth[3] + depth[0] + 1 = 3 + 0 + 1 = 4. Update min_cycle = 4. Continue DFS from 0: also path 0->3 (depth 1), 3->1 (depth 2), 1->2 (depth 3), 2->3 (depth 4) but 3 visited (parent 1) so cycle length = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7. Not shorter. Step 2: S
... (simplified)

Step 1: Start from vertex 0. DFS: 0->1 (depth 1), 1->2 (depth 2). No cycles found. Step 2: Start from vertex 1. DFS: 1->0 (depth 1), 1->2 (depth 1). No cycles found. Step 3: Start from vertex 2. DFS: 2->1 (depth 1), 1->0 (depth 2). No cycles found. All DFS complete, no cycles detected. Return -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS/BFS Cycle Detection
ladders.desc: Perform DFS from a given starting vertex to detect cycles and calculate their lengths.
ladders.explanation: This is the core DFS subroutine that detects cycles starting from a specific vertex. The intuition is that by performing depth-first search from a starting vertex, we can explore all paths emanating from it. When we encounter a vertex that has already been visited (and is not the immediate parent), we've found a cycle. We need to track the depth of each vertex during the DFS to calculate cycle lengths accurately. The algorithm: 1. Initialize visited array and depth array. 2. Start DFS from the given vertex with depth 0. 3. For each vertex u, explore all neighbors v: a. If v is not visited, recursively DFS with depth+1. b. If v is visited and v is not the parent of u, then we found a cycle. Calculate cycle length = depth[u] + depth[v] + 1. 4. Return the minimum cycle length found from this starting vertex. Time complexity is O(V + E) for the DFS traversal. Space complexity is O(V) for visited array, depth array, and recursion stack. This connects to L0 by being called for each vertex in the graph. Common mistakes include: not tracking depth correctly, forgetting to pass parent information, and incorrectly calculating cycle length (should add 1 for the edge between u and v). The real code pattern is a recursive DFS function with parent parameter.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start DFS at vertex 0, depth=0. Mark visited[0]=true, depth[0]=0. Step 2: Explore neighbors of 0: neighbor 1. 1 not visited, DFS(1, parent=0, depth=1). Mark visited[1]=true, depth[1]=1. Step 3: Explore neighbors of 1: neighbors 0 and 2. 0 is visited but is parent, ignore. 2 not visited, DFS(2, parent=1, depth=2). Mark visited[2]=true, depth[2]=2. Step 4: Explore neighbors of 2: neighbors 1 and 0. 1 is visited but is parent, ignore. 0 is visited and not parent (parent is 1), so cycle found! Cycle length = depth[2] + depth[0] + 1 = 2 + 0 + 1 = 3. Return 3.

Step 1: Start DFS at vertex 0, depth=0. Mark visited[0]=true, depth[0]=0. Step 2: Explore neighbors of 0: neighbor 1. 1 not visited, DFS(1, parent=0, depth=1). Mark visited[1]=true, depth[1]=1. Step 3: Explore neighbors of 1: neighbors 0 and 2. 0 is visited but is parent, ignore. 2 not visited, DFS(2, parent=1, depth=2). Mark visited[2]=true, depth[2]=2. Step 4: Explore neighbors of 2: neighbor 1. 1 is visited but is parent, ignore. No cycles found. Return -1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Cross Edge / Cycle Length Calculation
ladders.desc: The mechanism to identify when a cycle is found during DFS traversal.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight of cycle detection in undirected graphs using DFS. The intuition is simple: in an undirected graph, if during DFS we encounter a vertex that has already been visited AND it's not the vertex we just came from (the parent), then we've found a cycle. This works because in a tree (which is acyclic), the only visited neighbor you encounter is your parent. If you find another visited vertex, it means there's a back edge creating a cycle. The algorithm: During DFS traversal at vertex u, for each neighbor v: 1. If v is not visited, recursively DFS on v with u as parent. 2. If v is visited and v ≠ parent[u], then cycle detected. We need to track parent for each vertex to distinguish between going back along the same edge (which is not a cycle) versus finding a different path to an already-visited vertex (which indicates a cycle). Time complexity for this logic is O(1) per edge check. Space complexity is O(V) for parent array. This connects to L1 as the key condition check within DFS. Common mistakes include: not tracking parent correctly, treating all visited neighbors as cycles (including parent), and not handling the initial vertex specially (it has no parent). The real code pattern is checking 'if visited[v] and v != parent' during neighbor iteration.

examples.input: DFS at vertex 2, parent=1, neighbors [1,3,0], visited=[0,1,2,3], parent array tracking
examples.trace: Step 1: At vertex 2, parent is 1. Step 2: Check neighbor 1: visited=true, but 1 == parent, so ignore (this is just going back along same edge). Step 3: Check neighbor 3: visited=false, so would DFS into it (not shown). Step 4: Check neighbor 0: visited=true and 0 ≠ parent (parent is 1), so CYCLE DETECTED! This means there's a path from 2 to 0 that doesn't go through parent 1, indicating a cycle exists.

examples.input: DFS at vertex 1, parent=0, neighbors [0,2], visited=[0,1], parent array tracking
examples.trace: Step 1: At vertex 1, parent is 0. Step 2: Check neighbor 0: visited=true, and 0 == parent, so ignore (this is normal backtracking). Step 3: Check neighbor 2: visited=false, so would DFS into it. No cycle detected here because the only visited neighbor is the parent.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation
ladders.desc: Maintaining and using depth information during graph traversal.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves tracking how many edges we've traversed from the starting point during graph exploration. The intuition is that depth represents distance from the starting vertex in terms of edges traversed. When we find a cycle, we need to calculate its length, which requires knowing how far each vertex is from the start. The algorithm: 1. Initialize depth array with -1 (unvisited). 2. When visiting a vertex u, set depth[u] = current_depth. 3. When exploring neighbors, pass current_depth+1 to recursive calls. 4. When a cycle is detected between vertices u and v, the cycle length = depth[u] + depth[v] + 1. The '+1' accounts for the edge directly connecting u and v. Time complexity is O(1) per vertex for depth assignment. Space complexity is O(V) for depth array. This connects to L2 by providing the depth values needed for cycle length calculation. Common mistakes include: forgetting to initialize depth properly, not incrementing depth correctly during recursion, and off-by-one errors in cycle length calculation. The real code pattern is maintaining a depth array parallel to visited array.

examples.input: Starting depth=0 at vertex 0, graph traversal
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at vertex 0, depth[0]=0. Step 2: Go to neighbor 1, depth[1]=1. Step 3: From 1 go to neighbor 2, depth[2]=2. Step 4: From 2 go to neighbor 3, depth[3]=3. Step 5: From 3 go to neighbor 0 (already visited), depth[0]=0 already set. Cycle length calculation: depth[3] + depth[0] + 1 = 3 + 0 + 1 = 4. Final depth array: [0,1,2,3].

examples.input: Starting depth=0 at vertex 0, different traversal path
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at vertex 0, depth[0]=0. Step 2: Go to neighbor 2, depth[2]=1. Step 3: From 2 go to neighbor 1, depth[1]=2. Step 4: From 1 go to neighbor 3, depth[3]=3. Step 5: From 3 go to neighbor 0 (visited), depth[0]=0. Cycle length: depth[3] + depth[0] + 1 = 3 + 0 + 1 = 4. Same cycle length despite different traversal order. Depth array: [0,2,1,3].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Neighbor Iteration / Queue Operations
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of representing a graph and iterating through vertices and their neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational operation of working with graphs: how to represent them in code and how to access neighbors of a vertex. The intuition is that we need an efficient way to store which vertices are connected and to quickly find all neighbors of a given vertex. For undirected graphs, common representations are adjacency list (list of lists) or adjacency matrix. Adjacency list is preferred for sparse graphs. The algorithm: 1. Create an empty list of size V for adjacency list. 2. For each edge [u,v], add v to u's neighbor list and u to v's neighbor list (since undirected). 3. To iterate neighbors of vertex u, simply loop through adjacency_list[u]. Time complexity for building adjacency list is O(V + E). Space complexity is O(V + E). Accessing neighbors is O(degree(u)). This connects to L3 by providing the graph structure needed for traversal. Common mistakes include: forgetting to add both directions for undirected edges, using wrong indices, and not initializing the adjacency list properly. The real code pattern is a list of lists where graph[u] contains all neighbors of u.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize graph = [[],[],[],[]] for 4 vertices. Step 2: Process edge [0,1]: add 1 to graph[0], add 0 to graph[1]. graph = [[1],[0],[],[]]. Step 3: Process edge [1,2]: add 2 to graph[1], add 1 to graph[2]. graph = [[1],[0,2],[1],[]]. Step 4: Process edge [2,3]: add 3 to graph[2], add 2 to graph[3]. graph = [[1],[0,2],[1,3],[2]]. Step 5: Process edge [3,0]: add 0 to graph[3], add 3 to graph[0]. Final graph = [[1,3],[0,2],[1,3],[2,0]]. To iterate neighbors of vertex 1: graph[1] = [0,2], so neighbors are 0 and 2.

Step 1: Initialize graph = [[],[],[]]. Step 2: Process [0,1]: graph[0].append(1), graph[1].append(0). graph = [[1],[0],[]]. Step 3: Process [1,2]: graph[1].append(2), graph[2].append(1). Final graph = [[1],[0,2],[1]]. Neighbors of vertex 1: graph[1] = [0,2]. Neighbors of vertex 2: graph[2] = [1].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Cycles in Undirected Graphs
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of cycles in undirected graphs and detection methods.
ladders.explanation: A cycle in an undirected graph is a path that starts and ends at the same vertex without repeating edges. The shortest cycle is the one with the minimal number of edges. BFS or DFS from each vertex can detect and measure cycles efficiently.

examples.trace: Step 1: Identify vertices: 0, 1, 2. Step 2: Identify edges: 0 connected to 1, 1 connected to 2, 2 connected to 0. Step 3: Check for cycles: Start at vertex 0. Step 4: Go to vertex 1 (using edge 0-1). Step 5: Go to vertex 2 (using edge 1-2). Step 6: Go back to vertex 0 (using edge 2-0). Step 7: We returned to starting vertex 0 without repeating vertices or edges (except start/end). This is a cycle! Step 8: Count edges: 3 edges (0-1, 1-2, 2-0), so cycle length is 3.

Step 1: Vertices: 0, 1, 2. Step 2: Edges: 0-1 and 1-2. Step 3: Try to find a cycle: Start at 0, go to 1, go to 2. Cannot go anywhere else from 2. Step 4: Try starting at 1: can go to 0 or 2, but cannot return to 1 without repeating edge. Step 5: No matter where we start, we cannot return to starting vertex without repeating an edge. Therefore, no cycle exists. This graph is actually a path.

approaches.name: BFS from Each Node
approaches.summary: Run BFS from each vertex to detect cycles by checking for cross edges between nodes at the same level, which indicates a cycle. This method finds the shortest cycle by exploring levels incrementally from each start point.

ladders.title: Full Problem — BFS from Each Node
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of BFS from each vertex to find the shortest cycle by detecting cross edges between nodes at the same level.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that in an undirected graph, the shortest cycle containing a vertex v can be found by performing BFS starting from v. During BFS, if we encounter an edge connecting two nodes at the same level (a cross edge), this indicates we've found a cycle. The length of this cycle is 2*depth + 1, where depth is the level where the cross edge was found. By running BFS from every vertex and tracking the minimum cycle length found, we guarantee finding the shortest cycle in the graph. This works because BFS explores nodes in increasing order of distance from the source, so the first cycle found from a given source is the shortest cycle containing that source. The algorithm: 1. Initialize answer as INF. 2. For each vertex v: a. Perform BFS from v with distance array dist initialized to -1. b. During BFS, when exploring neighbor u of current node: if dist[u] == -1 (unvisited), set dist[u] = dist[current] + 1 and add to queue; else if dist[u] >= dist[current] (visited at same or higher level), we've found a cycle of length dist[current] + dist[u] + 1. Update answer if smaller. 3. Return answer or -1 if no cycle. Time: O(V*(V+E)) since BFS from each vertex. Space: O(V) for BFS queue and distance array. Connection to L1: This uses the BFS cycle detection subroutine from each starting node. Common mistakes: Not handling multiple components, forgetting to skip parent in cycle check (but cross edge detection handles this), incorrect cycle length calculation. Real code pattern: def shortest_cycle(graph): n = len(graph); ans = INF; for v in range(n): dist = [-1]*n; q = deque([v]); dist[v] = 0; while q: u = q.popleft(); for w in graph[u]: if dist[w] == -1: dist[w] = dist[u] + 1; q.append(w); elif dist[w] >= dist[u]: ans = min(ans, dist[u] + dist[w] + 1); return ans if ans != INF else -1.

examples.trace: Start BFS from node 0: dist[0]=0, queue=[0]. Process 0: neighbors 1,3,2. All unvisited: dist[1]=1, dist[3]=1, dist[2]=1; queue=[1,3,2]. Process 1: neighbor 0 visited at level 0 (dist[0]=0 < dist[1]=1, not cross edge), neighbor 2 visited at level 1 (dist[2]=1 >= dist[1]=1 → cross edge! cycle length = dist[1]+dist[2]+1 = 1+1+1=3). Update ans=3. Continue BFS but ans remains 3. BFS from other nodes won't find shorter. Return 3.

Graph is a path, no cycles. BFS from node 0: dist[0]=0. Process 0: neighbor 1 unvisited dist[1]=1. Process 1: neighbor 0 visited (dist[0]=0 < dist[1]=1), neighbor 2 unvisited dist[2]=2. Continue BFS, no cross edges found. Same for other starts. ans remains INF, return -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS/BFS Cycle Detection
ladders.desc: Perform BFS from a given source vertex to detect the shortest cycle containing that source by checking for cross edges.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that BFS explores nodes in layers by distance from the source. In an undirected graph, if during BFS we find an edge connecting two nodes at the same layer (same distance from source), this creates a cycle. The cycle length is 2*depth + 1 because both nodes are at distance depth from source, plus the edge between them. This gives the shortest cycle containing the source because BFS finds the shortest paths to all nodes, so the first such cross edge gives the minimal cycle. Algorithm: Input: graph, source s. 1. Initialize dist array with -1 (unvisited), dist[s]=0. 2. Initialize queue with s. 3. While queue not empty: a. u = dequeue. b. For each neighbor v of u: if dist[v] == -1: set dist[v] = dist[u] + 1, enqueue v; else if dist[v] >= dist[u]: cycle found! length = dist[u] + dist[v] + 1. Return min length found. Time: O(V+E) for BFS. Space: O(V). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each vertex in the full problem. Common mistakes: Using visited flag instead of distance, not handling the case where dist[v] == dist[u] (cross edge) correctly, forgetting to check dist[v] >= dist[u] (not just equal). Real code pattern: def bfs_cycle(graph, s): n=len(graph); dist=[-1]*n; dist[s]=0; q=deque([s]); ans=INF; while q: u=q.popleft(); for v in graph[u]: if dist[v]==-1: dist[v]=dist[u]+1; q.append(v); elif dist[v]>=dist[u]: ans=min(ans, dist[u]+dist[v]+1); return ans.

examples.trace: dist[0]=0, queue=[0]. Process 0: neighbors 1,2. Both unvisited: dist[1]=1, dist[2]=1; queue=[1,2]. Process 1: neighbor 0 visited (dist[0]=0 < dist[1]=1), neighbor 2 visited (dist[2]=1 >= dist[1]=1 → cross edge! length=1+1+1=3). Return 3.

dist[0]=0, queue=[0]. Process 0: neighbor 1 unvisited dist[1]=1; queue=[1]. Process 1: neighbor 0 visited (dist[0]=0 < dist[1]=1), neighbor 2 unvisited dist[2]=2; queue=[2]. Process 2: neighbor 1 visited (dist[1]=1 < dist[2]=2), neighbor 3 unvisited dist[3]=3; queue=[3]. Process 3: neighbor 2 visited (dist[2]=2 < dist[3]=3). No cross edges, return INF.

testCases.input: Graph: single node 0, no edges. Source: 0

ladders.title: Core Logic — Cross Edge / Cycle Length Calculation
ladders.desc: Identify cycles in undirected graphs by finding edges between nodes at the same BFS level.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that in BFS traversal of an undirected graph, edges can be classified as tree edges (connecting a node to an unvisited node) or cross edges (connecting two already visited nodes at the same or different levels). A cross edge between nodes at the same level creates an odd-length cycle: if both nodes are at distance d from the source, the path from source to each node (length d) plus the direct edge between them (length 1) forms a cycle of length 2d+1. This is the shortest cycle containing the source because BFS guarantees shortest paths, so d is minimal. Why it works: BFS explores in layers; when we process node u at level L, its neighbor v might already be visited at level M. If M == L, the edge u-v creates a cycle. If M < L, v is the parent of u (tree edge from earlier level). If M > L, impossible in BFS. So condition dist[v] >= dist[u] catches cross edges. Algorithm: During BFS, for current node u with neighbors: if neighbor v unvisited, it's tree edge; if visited and dist[v] >= dist[u], it's cross edge indicating cycle. Time: O(1) per edge check. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This logic is embedded in the BFS cycle detection subroutine. Common mistakes: Confusing with DFS cycle detection, not considering that in undirected graphs, the edge to parent should be ignored (but dist[parent] < dist[u], so condition dist[v] >= dist[u] automatically excludes parent). Real code pattern: In BFS loop: for v in graph[u]: if dist[v]==-1: tree edge; else if dist[v]>=dist[u]: cross edge → cycle.

examples.input: BFS from source 0, current node u=1 at level 1, neighbor v=2 at level 1
examples.trace: During BFS, dist[1]=1, processing u=1. Check neighbor v=2. dist[2] is already set to 1 (visited). Since dist[2]=1 >= dist[1]=1, this is a cross edge at same level. Cycle length = dist[1]+dist[2]+1 = 1+1+1=3.

examples.input: BFS from source 0, current node u=2 at level 2, neighbor v=1 at level 1
examples.trace: dist[2]=2, processing u=2. Check neighbor v=1. dist[1]=1 (visited). Since dist[1]=1 < dist[2]=2, this is the edge to parent (tree edge from when v was visited earlier). Condition dist[v]>=dist[u] is false, so no cycle detected.

testCases.input: dist[u]=0, dist[v]=0 (self-loop? but undirected graph typically no self-edges)
testCases.expected: Invalid in undirected, but would be cycle length 1

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation
ladders.desc: Perform breadth-first search from a source node while recording the distance (level) of each node from the source.
ladders.explanation: BFS is a graph traversal algorithm that explores nodes in layers based on their distance from the source. The intuition is to visit all nodes at distance 1, then distance 2, etc., ensuring we find the shortest path to each node. This is achieved using a queue: start with source at distance 0, then repeatedly dequeue a node, visit its unvisited neighbors, set their distance to current distance +1, and enqueue them. Distance tracking is crucial because it allows us to know how far each node is from the source, which is needed to detect cycles via cross edges. Algorithm: Input: graph, source s. 1. Initialize dist array of size V with -1 (meaning unvisited). 2. Set dist[s] = 0. 3. Initialize queue with s. 4. While queue not empty: a. u = dequeue(). b. For each neighbor v of u: if dist[v] == -1: dist[v] = dist[u] + 1; enqueue(v). 5. Return dist array. Time: O(V+E) as each node and edge processed once. Space: O(V) for dist and queue. Connection to L2: This BFS with distances provides the dist values used in cross edge detection. Common mistakes: Forgetting to mark source as visited, using visited boolean instead of distance (loses level info), not handling disconnected graphs. Real code pattern: def bfs_distances(graph, s): n=len(graph); dist=[-1]*n; dist[s]=0; q=deque([s]); while q: u=q.popleft(); for v in graph[u]: if dist[v]==-1: dist[v]=dist[u]+1; q.append(v); return dist.

examples.trace: Start: dist=[-1,-1,-1], dist[0]=0, queue=[0]. Process 0: neighbors [1]. dist[1]==-1 → dist[1]=dist[0]+1=1, queue=[1]. Process 1: neighbors [0,2]. dist[0]=0 (visited), dist[2]==-1 → dist[2]=dist[1]+1=2, queue=[2]. Process 2: neighbors [1]. dist[1]=1 (visited). Queue empty. Return dist=[0,1,2].

dist=[-1,-1,-1,-1], dist[0]=0, queue=[0]. Process 0: neighbors [1,2]. Both unvisited: dist[1]=1, dist[2]=1; queue=[1,2]. Process 1: neighbors [0,3]. dist[0]=0 (visited), dist[3]==-1 → dist[3]=dist[1]+1=2; queue=[2,3]. Process 2: neighbors [0]. dist[0]=0 (visited). Process 3: neighbors [1]. dist[1]=1 (visited). Return dist=[0,1,1,2].

testCases.input: Graph: single node 0, no edges. Source: 0

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Neighbor Iteration / Queue Operations
ladders.desc: Use a queue data structure to visit nodes in first-in-first-out order, processing all nodes at current level before moving to next.
ladders.explanation: A queue is a FIFO (first-in, first-out) data structure that supports enqueue (add to back) and dequeue (remove from front). In graph traversal, using a queue ensures we visit nodes in the order they are discovered, which corresponds to level-order: all nodes at distance d from the source are processed before nodes at distance d+1. This is the essence of BFS. The intuition is that by queuing nodes as we discover them, we naturally process them in increasing distance order. Algorithm for level-order traversal from source: 1. Create empty queue. 2. Mark source as visited, enqueue source. 3. While queue not empty: a. Dequeue node u. b. Process u (e.g., print or store). c. For each unvisited neighbor v of u: mark visited, enqueue v. This guarantees that nodes are visited in order of their distance from source. Time: O(V+E) if using adjacency list. Space: O(V) for queue. Connection to L3: This queue mechanism is used in BFS to track nodes to visit, enabling distance tracking. Common mistakes: Using stack instead of queue (gives DFS), not marking nodes as visited before enqueuing (causes reprocessing), forgetting to check for empty queue. Real code pattern: from collections import deque; def level_order(graph, start): visited=set([start]); q=deque([start]); while q: u=q.popleft(); print(u); for v in graph[u]: if v not in visited: visited.add(v); q.append(v).

examples.trace: Queue=[0], visited={0}. Dequeue 0, print 0. Neighbors 1,2: both unvisited → add to visited, enqueue: queue=[1,2]. Dequeue 1, print 1. Neighbors 0,3: 0 visited, 3 unvisited → visited add 3, enqueue: queue=[2,3]. Dequeue 2, print 2. Neighbors 0: visited. Dequeue 3, print 3. Neighbors 1: visited. Queue empty.

Queue=[0], visited={0}. Dequeue 0, print 0. Neighbors 1,2: both unvisited → visited add 1,2, enqueue: queue=[1,2]. Dequeue 1, print 1. Neighbors 0,2: 0 visited, 2 visited. Dequeue 2, print 2. Neighbors 0,1: both visited. Queue empty.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Cycles in Undirected Graphs
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of cycles in undirected graphs and detection methods.
ladders.explanation: A cycle in an undirected graph is a path that starts and ends at the same vertex without repeating edges. The shortest cycle is the one with the minimal number of edges. BFS or DFS from each vertex can detect and measure cycles efficiently.

examples.trace: Initialize graph = [[], [], []]. Add edge 0-1: graph[0].append(1); graph[1].append(0) → graph=[[1],[0],[]]. Add edge 1-2: graph[1].append(2); graph[2].append(1) → graph=[[1],[0,2],[1]].

approaches.name: Union-Find with BFS
approaches.summary: Use Union-Find to detect when adding an edge creates a cycle, then run BFS from one endpoint of that edge to find the shortest cycle involving it. This combines cycle detection with shortest path search for efficiency.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Union-Find with BFS
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find the shortest cycle in an undirected graph using Union-Find for cycle detection and BFS for path finding.
ladders.explanation: This approach efficiently finds the shortest cycle by combining two classic algorithms. The intuition is that when we add edges incrementally, the first time Union-Find detects a cycle, we know we've found two nodes already connected—meaning there exists at least one cycle. However, Union-Find alone doesn't give us the cycle length. By running BFS from one endpoint of the newly added edge to the other (excluding that direct edge), we find the shortest path between them through other edges, which when combined with the direct edge forms the shortest cycle involving that edge. The overall shortest cycle in the graph will be discovered when processing edges in any order, as we check each potential cycle as soon as it's created. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize Union-Find for all nodes. 2) For each edge (u,v) in the graph: a) If find(u) == find(v), run BFS from u to v to get shortest path length d, then cycle length = d+1. Update global minimum. b) Else, union(u,v). 3) Return the minimum cycle length found or -1 if no cycle. Time complexity is O(V + E*α(V)) for Union-Find operations plus O(V+E) for each BFS in worst case, but in practice BFS runs only when cycles are detected. Space is O(V) for Union-Find and BFS queues. Connection to L1: This integrates the key subroutine of BFS path finding with Union-Find cycle detection. Common mistakes include not excluding the direct edge in BFS (leading to incorrect cycle length of 1), forgetting to handle disconnected graphs, and not updating the global minimum correctly. Real code pattern: Initialize parent array for Union-Find, adjacency list for graph. Process edges: if connected, BFS; else union and add edge to adjacency list.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize Union-Find parent=[0,1,2,3], graph adjacency={}. Step 2: Process edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, different, union(0,1), parent=[0,0,2,3], add edge to graph: {0:[1],1:[0]}. Step 3: Process edge [1,2]: find(1)=0, find(2)=2, different, union(0,2), parent=[0,0,0,3], add edge: {0:[1],1:[0,2],2:[1]}. Step 4: Process edge [2,3]: find(2)=0, find(3)=3, different, union(0,3), parent=[0,0,0,0], add edge: {0:[1],1:[0,2],2:[1,3],3:[2]}. Step 5: Process edge [3,0]: find(3)=0, find(0)=0, same, so cycle detected. Run BFS from 3 to 0 exclud
... (simplified)

Step 1: Initialize parent=[0,1,2], graph={}. Step 2: Process [0,1]: find different, union(0,1), parent=[0,0,2], graph={0:[1],1:[0]}. Step 3: Process [1,2]: find(1)=0, find(2)=2, different, union(0,2), parent=[0,0,0], graph={0:[1],1:[0,2],2:[1]}. Step 4: Process [2,0]: find(2)=0, find(0)=0, same, cycle detected. BFS from 2 to 0 excluding edge [2,0]. Adjacency: 2 connected to 1, 0 connected to 1. BFS: start 2 dist0, visit 1 dist1, from 1 visit 0 dist2. Found 0 at distance 2, cycle length=2+1=3. Output: 3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS/BFS Cycle Detection
ladders.desc: Implement BFS to compute the shortest path length between two given nodes in an undirected graph, excluding a specific edge.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is crucial because once Union-Find detects a cycle, we need to find the shortest path between the two endpoints of the edge that created the cycle, excluding that direct edge. The intuition is that in an undirected graph, BFS naturally finds the shortest path in terms of number of edges when all edges have equal weight. By excluding the direct edge between the start and target nodes, we ensure we find a path through other nodes, which when combined with the direct edge forms a cycle. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize a queue with the start node, distance 0, and a visited set. 2) While queue is not empty: dequeue a node, if it's the target, return its distance. 3) Otherwise, for each neighbor not visited and not the direct excluded edge, enqueue with distance+1. 4) If queue empties without finding target, return infinity (no path). Time complexity is O(V+E) for the BFS traversal. Space is O(V) for queue and visited set. Connection to L0: This BFS is called whenever Union-Find detects a cycle to compute the cycle length. Common mistakes include not excluding the direct edge (which would give distance 0 and incorrect cycle length 1), not marking nodes visited (causing infinite loops), and using DFS instead of BFS (which doesn't guarantee shortest path). Real code pattern: Use deque for queue, store (node, dist). Check if neighbor == target and edge is excluded to handle cases where multiple edges exist.

examples.input: Graph: edges [[0,1],[1,2],[2,3],[3,0]], start=3, target=0, exclude edge [3,0]
examples.trace: Adjacency: 0:[1,3], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2,0]. Exclude edge [3,0]. BFS: queue starts with (3,0). Visit 3: neighbors 2 and 0. Edge [3,0] is excluded, so only enqueue 2 with dist1. Queue: (2,1). Visit 2: neighbors 1 and 3. 3 visited, enqueue 1 with dist2. Queue: (1,2). Visit 1: neighbors 0 and 2. 2 visited, enqueue 0 with dist3. Queue: (0,3). Visit 0: it is target, return dist=3.

examples.input: Graph: edges [[0,1],[1,2],[2,0]], start=2, target=0, exclude edge [2,0]
examples.trace: Adjacency: 0:[1,2], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,0]. Exclude [2,0]. BFS: start (2,0). Visit 2: neighbors 1 and 0. Exclude edge to 0, so enqueue 1 with dist1. Queue: (1,1). Visit 1: neighbors 0 and 2. 2 visited, enqueue 0 with dist2. Queue: (0,2). Visit 0: target, return 2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Cross Edge / Cycle Length Calculation
ladders.desc: Use Union-Find to detect when adding an edge connects two already-connected nodes, indicating a cycle.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that in an undirected graph, a cycle is formed when we add an edge between two nodes that are already in the same connected component. Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) is perfect for this because it efficiently tracks connectivity as edges are added. The intuition: initially, each node is its own component. When we add an edge (u,v), we check if find(u) == find(v). If yes, they're already connected, so adding this edge creates a cycle. If no, we union their components. This works because Union-Find maintains transitive connectivity—if u and v are connected indirectly through other edges, they'll have the same root. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize parent array where parent[i]=i. 2) For each edge (u,v): if find(u) == find(v), cycle detected; else union(u,v). Time complexity is O(E * α(V)) where α is the inverse Ackermann function, effectively constant. Space is O(V). Connection to L1: Once a cycle is detected, we use BFS to find its length. Common mistakes include not using path compression or union by rank (leading to worse performance), misinterpreting cycle detection (it detects any cycle, not necessarily the shortest first), and not handling edge cases like self-loops. Real code pattern: Implement find with path compression, union with rank. Process edges sequentially.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize parent=[0,1,2], rank=[0,0,0]. Step 2: Process [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, different, union(0,1). Parent becomes [0,0,2], rank [1,0,0]. Step 3: Process [1,2]: find(1)=0, find(2)=2, different, union(0,2). Parent [0,0,0], rank [1,0,0]. Step 4: Process [2,0]: find(2)=0, find(0)=0, same, so cycle detected.

Step 1: parent=[0,1,2,3]. Step 2: [0,1]: find different, union, parent=[0,0,2,3]. Step 3: [1,2]: find(1)=0, find(2)=2, different, union, parent=[0,0,0,3]. Step 4: [2,3]: find(2)=0, find(3)=3, different, union, parent=[0,0,0,0]. No cycle detected.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation
ladders.desc: Implement the Union-Find data structure with path compression and union by rank.
ladders.explanation: Union-Find is a data structure that maintains a collection of disjoint sets and supports two operations: find (determine which set an element belongs to) and union (merge two sets). The intuition is to represent each set as a tree, where each node points to its parent, and the root represents the set identifier. Path compression flattens the tree during find operations by making nodes point directly to the root, speeding up future finds. Union by rank attaches the shorter tree under the taller one to keep trees balanced, ensuring efficient operations. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize parent array where parent[i] = i, and rank array with zeros. 2) find(x): if parent[x] != x, set parent[x] = find(parent[x]) recursively (path compression), return parent[x]. 3) union(x,y): find roots rx and ry, if rx == ry, they're already united. Else, if rank[rx] < rank[ry], set parent[rx] = ry. If rank[rx] > rank[ry], set parent[ry] = rx. If equal, choose one as parent and increment its rank. Time complexity: nearly O(1) per operation due to α(n). Space: O(n). Connection to L2: This data structure is used to detect cycles by checking connectivity before uniting nodes. Common mistakes include not initializing parent correctly, forgetting path compression (leading to O(n) time), and not using union by rank (causing unbalanced trees). Real code pattern: Use arrays for parent and rank, implement find as recursive or iterative with path compression.

examples.trace: Initialize parent=[0,1,2], rank=[0,0,0]. union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, rank same, set parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. Parent=[0,0,2]. union(1,2): find(1)=0 (with path compression), find(2)=2, rank[0]=1 > rank[2]=0, set parent[2]=0. Parent=[0,0,0]. find(0): returns 0. find(2): parent[2]=0, so returns 0 after path compression.

examples.output: All nodes in same set with root 0
examples.trace: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3], rank=[0,0,0,0]. union(0,1): parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. union(2,3): parent[3]=2, rank[2]=1. union(0,2): find(0)=0, find(2)=2, rank both 1, set parent[2]=0, rank[0]=2. Final parent=[0,0,0,2] but after find(3), path compression may update.

testCases.input: Initialize 4 nodes, operations: union(0,1), find(0), find(1)
Initialize 5 nodes, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(0,2), find(3)
Initialize 3 nodes, operations: union(0,1), union(1,2), find(2)
Initialize 2 nodes, operations: union(0,1), union(0,1) again
Initialize 6 nodes, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(0,2), union(0,4), find(5)
Initialize 3 nodes, operations: union(1,2), union(0,1), find(2)
Initialize 4 nodes, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), find(0), find(2)
Initialize 5 nodes, operations: union(0,1), union(1,2), union(3,4), union(2,3), find(4)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Neighbor Iteration / Queue Operations
ladders.desc: Perform BFS on an undirected graph to visit all nodes level by level.
ladders.explanation: BFS is a graph traversal algorithm that explores nodes in layers: starting from a source node, it visits all neighbors at the present depth before moving to nodes at the next depth level. The intuition is to use a queue to maintain the frontier of nodes to visit, ensuring we process nodes in the order they're discovered, which guarantees the shortest path in unweighted graphs. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize a queue with the start node and a visited set. 2) While queue is not empty: dequeue a node, mark it visited, process it (e.g., print or check condition). 3) For each unvisited neighbor, enqueue it and mark visited. 4) Continue until queue is empty. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each node and edge is processed once. Space is O(V) for queue and visited set. Connection to L3: BFS is used in Union-Find approach to find shortest paths once cycles are detected. Common mistakes include not marking nodes visited when enqueuing (causing duplicates and infinite loops), using a stack instead of queue (turning into DFS), and not handling disconnected graphs. Real code pattern: Use collections.deque for queue, set for visited, adjacency list for graph.

examples.trace: Adjacency: 0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2]. Queue start: [0]. Visit 0: neighbors 1, enqueue 1. Queue: [1]. Visit 1: neighbors 0 (visited), 2, enqueue 2. Queue: [2]. Visit 2: neighbors 1 (visited), 3, enqueue 3. Queue: [3]. Visit 3: neighbors 2 (visited). Queue empty, done.

Adjacency: 0:[1,2], 1:[0,2], 2:[0,1]. Queue: [0]. Visit 0: neighbors 1,2, enqueue both. Queue: [1,2]. Visit 1: neighbors 0 (visited), 2 (already in queue or visited), no new enqueue. Visit 2: neighbors 0,1 both visited. Done.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Cycles in Undirected Graphs
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of cycles in undirected graphs and detection methods.
ladders.explanation: A cycle in an undirected graph is a path that starts and ends at the same vertex without repeating edges. The shortest cycle is the one with the minimal number of edges. BFS or DFS from each vertex can detect and measure cycles efficiently.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=3, initialize graph = [[], [], []]. Step 2: Process edge [0,1]: add 1 to graph[0], so graph[0]=[1]; add 0 to graph[1], so graph[1]=[0]. Step 3: Process edge [1,2]: add 2 to graph[1], so graph[1]=[0,2]; add 1 to graph[2], so graph[2]=[1]. Final: graph[0]=[1], graph[1]=[0,2], graph[2]=[1].
